name: Grail as Bloodline
description: The "Grail as Bloodline" theory, popularized in France and later globally, posits that the Holy Grail is not a physical object, but rather a bloodline descended from Jesus Christ and Mary Magdalene. This bloodline, according to proponents, continued through the Merovingian dynasty of Frankish kings. The theory suggests that the true secret of the Grail is the knowledge of this lineage and the power or spiritual significance it supposedly holds. Its origins can be traced to esoteric interpretations of Arthurian legends and the Rennes-le-Château mystery in southern France. Key evidence cited includes interpretations of medieval texts, alleged coded messages in artwork (particularly those associated with Nicolas Poussin), and the historical theories presented in the book "Holy Blood, Holy Grail" by Michael Baigent, Richard Leigh, and Henry Lincoln. Mainstream scholarly perspectives largely dismiss the theory as pseudohistorical. Historians point out inaccuracies and misinterpretations in the evidence presented, emphasizing the lack of credible historical documentation to support the claim of a Jesus bloodline. They argue that the theory relies on selective readings of historical sources and a willingness to accept unsubstantiated claims. The connection to the Merovingians is also debated, with historians emphasizing the lack of evidence linking them to a Jesus lineage.
subtitle: Jesus bloodline secret and Merovingian dynasty

claims.claim: The Holy Grail is a bloodline descended from Jesus and Mary Magdalene.
claims.evidence: Interpretations of the Gospels, Apocryphal texts, and alleged secret societies.
claims.criticism: Lack of historical evidence, misinterpretations of religious texts, and unsubstantiated claims about secret societies.

claims.claim: The Merovingian dynasty are direct descendants of Jesus and Mary Magdalene.
claims.evidence: Genealogical interpretations, alleged links to the Tribe of Benjamin, and coded messages in historical documents.
claims.criticism: Genealogical claims are unsubstantiated, historical documents are misinterpreted, and no reliable evidence connects the Merovingians to Jesus.

claims.claim: The Priory of Sion protects the secret of the Grail bloodline.
claims.evidence: Claims of a secret society documented in "Le Serpent Rouge" and other questionable sources.
claims.criticism: The Priory of Sion has been widely debunked as a hoax created by Pierre Plantard.

keyFigures.name: Michael Baigent, Richard Leigh, Henry Lincoln
keyFigures.role: Authors of "Holy Blood, Holy Grail", popularizing the theory.

keyFigures.name: Pierre Plantard
keyFigures.role: Claimed to be a Grand Master of the Priory of Sion.

keyFigures.name: Dan Brown
keyFigures.role: Author of 'The Da Vinci Code' which popularized the theory.

keyFigures.name: Jean-Luc Chaumeil
keyFigures.role: Researcher and author who has debunked much of the Rennes-le-Château mythology.

timeline.event: Emergence of Grail romances in Arthurian literature.
The Rennes-le-Château mystery begins to develop.
Pierre Plantard revives the Priory of Sion myth.

sources.author: Baigent, Michael; Leigh, Richard; Lincoln, Henry
